parsed.title: Foundational Probabilities: Information Theory and Markov Models
parsed.scope: This section introduces language as a sequence of probabilistic events rather than strict grammatical rules. It covers Shannon Entropy from first principles as a mathematical measure of information and uncertainty, and introduces Markov Chains and n-gram models as the earliest statistical frameworks for sequence prediction.

parsed.title: Information Theory, Entropy, and the Birth of Generative Language Models
parsed.scope: Introduce the mathematical definition of information and Shannon Entropy from a first-principles perspective. Connect this mathematical foundation to the earliest probabilistic representations of language using Markov Chains and n-gram models, demonstrating how text generation is fundamentally a stochastic process.

parsed.title: Quantifying and Generating Language: Entropy and Markov Models
parsed.scope: This section establishes the first principles of probabilistic language modeling. It begins by defining how information is mathematically quantified using Shannon Entropy, then explores how sequence generation is modeled using Markov Chains and n-grams within a generative, rather than discriminative, framework.
